assertiveness_why: Harry asks several pointed, skeptic-flavored questions (unit economics, profitability timelines, whether markets show loyalty or fragmentation) but frames them as genuine curiosity rather than contesting Brad's answers, and accepts each response without follow-up pushback.
question_style: Structured, well-researched questions that move logically from origin story to competitive landscape to unit economics to team and culture; he directly names investor skepticism ('there are some skeptics around... the unit economics') as a premise but doesn't press further once Brad answers.
tone: Professional, admiring, and enthusiastic, with light personal color (jokes about Brad's grueling daily schedule and his youthful appearance), staying deferential throughout.
depth: Goes reasonably deep on operational specifics: multi-market profitability drivers, hardware iteration cycles and the classic cost/durability/feature tradeoff, and go-global-vs-focus strategic tension, though follow-ups are generally single-layer rather than compounding.
harry_quotes: How do you view the landscape today from your mindset?
What's the record time to get to profitability?
learnings: Lime treats hardware iteration as a core competitive advantage, releasing four major vehicle generations in twelve months versus a typical two-to-five-year industry cycle.

In physical hardware products you generally can only optimize two of three variables (best experience, durability, lowest cost) at once, and Lime is trying to push on all three simultaneously as a deliberate differentiator.
Brad frames micro-mobility's addressable market as still under 1% of urban short trips, arguing the opportunity is expanding the category rather than fighting competitors for share.
Lime deliberately skipped the common 'launch first, negotiate with the city later' playbook used by ride-hailing, opting for upfront regulatory engagement instead.
Trust between co-founders built over years prior to founding (in this case an eight-to-nine-year prior relationship) was cited as the key ingredient enabling fast, low-friction major decisions.
notable: Brad reveals Lime's fastest market reached profitability in just two weeks, a striking and unexpected data point Harry visibly wasn't anticipating.
